section: ABSTRACT
text: Two rods of standard Hastelloy N were removed from the surveillance position outside the core vessel and were exposed to the nitrogen plus 2 to $5\%$ $\mathsf{O}_2$ cell environment for 11,000 hr.

section: ABSTRACT
text: These samples were exposed to a thermal fluence of $1.3\times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm², and the mechanical properties were altered appreciably.

section: ABSTRACT
text: Two rods of modified Hastelloy N containing small additions of titanium and zirconium were removed from the core surveillance facility with a thermal fluence of $4.1 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².

section: INTRODUCTION
text: The Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment is a single-region reactor that is fueled by a molten fluoride salt (65 LiF-29.1 BeF $_2$ -5 ZrF $_4$ -0.9 UF $_4$ , mole %), moderated by unclad graphite, and contained by Hastelloy N (Ni-16 Mo-7 Cr-4 Fe-0.05 C, wt %).

section: INTRODUCTION
text: and construction can be found elsewhere. $^{1}$ We knew that the neutron environment would produce some changes in the two structural materials - graphite and Hastelloy N, and we were very confident of the compatibility of these materials with the fluoride salt.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: The reactor went critical on June 1, 1965, and assumed normal operation in May 1966.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: The first group of surveillance specimens was in the reactor from September 8, 1965, to July 28, 1966, and was removed after 8682 Mwhr of operation (designated "first group").

section: INTRODUCTION
text: A second set of specimens was removed from the core on May 9, 1967, after an additional 27,600 Mwhr of operation (total reactor operation was 36,247 Mwhr).

section: INTRODUCTION
text: These specimens were two modified alloys containing nominal $0.5\%$ additions of titanium and zirconium.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: These were inserted on September 13, 1966, after the first group was removed to determine the mechanical property changes of these alloys due to irradiation and to evaluate the compatibility of these alloys with the MSRE environment.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: Two rods of standard Hastelloy N located outside the core and 5 in. from the vessel were removed on June 5, 1967.

section: INTRODUCTION
text: These specimens had been in place since the reactor began operation and were examined to determine the compatibility of the material with the MSRE cell environment $(\mathsf{N}_2 + 2 - 5\% \mathsf{O}_2)$ and the changes in the mechanical properties due to irradiation.

section: Surveillance Assembly
text: Cook and others, and the details have been reported.<sup>3</sup> The facility is shown pictorially and schematically in Fig.

section: Surveillance Assembly
text: Each stringer is about 62 in. long and consists of two Hastelloy N rods and a graphite section made up of various pieces that are joined by pinning and tongue-and-groove joints.

section: Surveillance Assembly
text: The Hastelloy N rod is periodically reduced to 0.125 in. in diameter and can be cut into small tensile specimens after it is removed from the reactor.

section: Surveillance Assembly
text: The assembled stringers fit into a perforated Hastelloy N basket that is inserted into an axial position about 2.8 in. from the core center line.

section: Surveillance Assembly
text: There is another surveillance facility for Hastelloy N located outside the core in a vertical position about 5 in. from the vessel (Fig.

section: Surveillance Assembly
text: These specimens are exposed to the cell environment $(\mathsf{N}_2 + 2 - 5\% \mathsf{O}_2)$ .

section: Materials
text: The compositions of the two heats of standard Hastelloy N are given in Table 1.

section: Materials
text: Heat 5085 was used for making the cylindrical portion, and heat 5065 was used for the top and bottom heads of the MSRE vessel.

section: Materials
text: These materials were given a mill anneal of 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a final anneal of 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at ORNL after fabrication.

section: Ornl-Dwg 68-8298
Heats 5065 and 5085
text: Materials available as 1 l/8- and 9/16-in. plate, respectively.

section: Heats 5065 and 5085
text: Mill annealed 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: Heats 5065 and 5085
text: Annealed 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: Heat 21545
text: Rods were annealed 100 hr at $871^{\circ}C$

section: Heat 21554
text: Plate worked at $871^{\circ}C$ to 0.3 in. thickness.

section: Heat 21554
text: Rods were annealed 100 hr at $871^{\circ}C$

section: Heat 21554
text: Rods reannealed 100 hr at $871^{\circ}C$

section: Heat 21554
text: Both procedures resulted in a very fine grain size (ASTM grain size 7 to 9).

section: Test Specimens
text: The surveillance rods inside the core are 62 in. long and those outside the vessel are 81 in. long.

section: Test Specimens
text: They both are $\frac{1}{4}$ in. in diameter with reduced sections $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{8}$ in. long.

section: Test Specimens
text: After removal from the reactor, the rods are sawed into small mechanical property specimens having a gage section $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{8}$ in. long.

section: Test Specimens
text: The first rods were machined in small segments (approx 11 l/2 in. long) and welded together.

section: Test Specimens
text: A milling machine is used with a cutter ground to the shape of the gage length, including the radius where the gage length blends into the full $1/4$ in. diameter of the rod.

section: Testing Techniques
text: The temperature accuracy was $\pm 0.75\%$ , the guaranteed accuracy of the Chromel-P-Alumel thermocouples used.

text: extensometer (mechanical and electrical portions) produced measurements that could be read to about $\pm 0.02\%$ strain; however, other factors (temperature changes in the cell, mechanical vibrations, etc.) probably combined to give an overall accuracy of $\pm 0.1\%$ strain.

text: This error and the thermocouple accuracy combine to give a temperature uncertainty of about $\pm 1\%$ .

text: A Comparison of the Ductility of Surveillance Specimens of Heat 5085 Exposed in the Core and Outside the Core Vessel.

section: Mechanical Property Data - Standard Hastelloy N
text: At a strain rate of $0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ , the fracture strain is decreased appreciably as the thermal fluence increased from $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ to $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .

section: Mechanical Property Data - Standard Hastelloy N
text: At a slower strain rate of $0.002 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ , the same trend holds, but the change is much smaller.

section: Mechanical Property Data - Standard Hastelloy N
text: Our previous studies showed that the tensile properties of materials irradiated in the MSRE and those irradiated in the ORR and ETR in a helium environment were similar, the only significant difference being the reduction in fracture strain observed at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: Mechanical Property Data - Standard Hastelloy N
text: Our present studies also support previous observations that the yield stress was unaffected by irradiation and that the ultimate tensile stress was reduced about $10\%$ at low temperatures and progressively reduced further above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to a maximum of $30\%$ at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: The standard Hastelloy N used in constructing the MSRE continues to show excellent compatibility with the fluoride salt environment and the cell environment consisting of $\mathbb{N}_2 - 2$ to $5\% 0_2$ .

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: There was no evidence of nitriding, and the depth of oxidation in 11,000 hr was only a few mils.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: We knew from previous studies that the mechanical properties of Hastelloy N would deteriorate due to irradiation damage.[12-17] Our examination of the first group of surveillance specimens[18] showed that the property changes were as-predicted, the only exception being a reduction in the ductility at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: Extraction replicas showed that extensive carbide precipitation occurred along the grain boundaries during irradiation at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and this probably accounts for the reduction in ductility at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: receives a thermal flux about a factor of 40 higher than that received by the vessel.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: The first surveillance specimens removed from the core received a thermal fluence of $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² while being heated for 4800 hr.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: The surveillance specimens of standard Hastelloy N removed with the second group were located outside the core and received a thermal fluence of $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm² while being heated for 11,000 hr.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: 25) is reduced even more by the higher fluence.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: In creep-rupture tests at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Fig.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: 27), the rupture life is also slightly shorter and the strain at fracture (Fig.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: 29) slightly less for the specimen irradiated to the higher fluence.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: In the unirradiated condition, heat 5085 exhibited fracture strains of 20 to $40\%$ in creep-rupture tests compared with fracture strains of about 2.2 and $1.5\%$ after irradiation to thermal fluences of $1.3 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm² and $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm², respectively.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: The modified alloys, heats 21554 (zirconium modified) and 21545 (titanium modified), were annealed to produce a small grain size.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: Since these samples were placed in the MSRE, we have found that material annealed to produce a coarser grain size has better properties after irradiation.[19] Thus, we were not surprised that the postirradiation

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: However, two encouraging observations were made that demonstrate the absence of embrittling aging reactions: (1) the mechanical properties of the control specimens exposed to salt for 5500 hr were only slightly different from those of the as-annealed material (Figs.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: 38 and 41) and (2) the strain at fracture in tensile tests at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was not reduced by irradiation.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: Our measurements $^{20}$ of the lattice diffusion of titanium in the modified alloy indicate that the gradients in these samples should extend to a depth of only about 0.15 mil (based on a lattice diffusion coefficient of $1 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{cm}^2/\text{sec}$ and 5500 hr exposure at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: However, titanium can diffuse more rapidly along the grain boundaries and we can approximate this depth of removal by the Fisher model. $^{21}$ Assuming that the grain boundary diffusion rate is $10^6$ times that for the lattice, $^{22}$ significant depletion of the titanium should occur along the grain boundaries to depths of about 4 mils.

section: DISCUSSION OF RESULTS
text: 51 and 57) were exposed to static salt but did exhibit edge cracking when tested.

section: SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
text: surveillance position outside the core after $11,000\mathrm{hr}$ at temperature with a thermal fluence of $1.3\times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm².

section: SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
text: The compatibility of the material with the cell environment of nitrogen plus 2 to $5\%$ $\mathsf{O}_2$ seems excellent with no evidence of nitriding and only superficial oxidation.

section: SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
text: Mechanical property tests show that the fracture strain at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was reduced markedly by irradiation.

section: SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
text: Creep-rupture tests at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ show a reduction in rupture life and ductility.

section: SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
text: A comparison with results from the first group of surveillance specimens exposed to the core environment to a fluence of $1.3\times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm² indicated that the mechanical properties deteriorated only slightly with increasing fluence.

section: SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS
text: Two heats of modified Hastelloy N containing $0.5\%$ Ti and $0.5\%$ Zr were removed from the core with a thermal fluence of $4.1 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².
